serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Mee Goreng Malay Style typically weighs approximately 350 g, as commonly served at hawker centres and zi char restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Malay Style) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for Mee Goreng (Malay Style) and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for yellow noodle stir-fry dishes. Per-100g values are derived from the composite dish profile. The restaurant version (Marina Seafood House, Boat Quay) may differ in exact proportions; values represent a standard Malay-style mee goreng preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Yellow Wheat Noodles and Stir-Fried Noodle Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Goreng Malay Style. GI estimated at 50 based on yellow wheat noodles (GI ~50, low category) as the primary carbohydrate source, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled/stir-fried wheat noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 68.25) / 100 ≈ 34.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 19.5 × 3.5 = 68.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mee Goreng Malay Style to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and seasoning, as a single serving can contribute approximately 64% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~34 per serving) from yellow wheat noodles and added sugars from ketchup and sauces
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Mee Goreng Malay Style in controlled portions, as the moderate glycemic load of approximately 34 per standard serving can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and reducing sauce quantity can help blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content with stir-frying oil contributing to overall energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Mee Goreng Malay Style should be consumed in moderation due to its high sodium content (~1470 mg per serving) and moderate fat contribution (~20 g per serving), which together may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; choosing preparations with less oil and reduced-sodium soy sauce is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Goreng Malay Style (Mee Goreng Melayu) is a popular Southeast Asian stir-fried noodle dish originating from Malay culinary tradition and widely consumed across Malaysia and Singapore. The dish is prepared by wok-frying yellow wheat noodles at high heat with egg, firm tofu, potato cubes, bean sprouts, tomato, onion, garlic, fresh chilli, soy sauce, and tomato ketchup, producing a characteristic sweet-savoury-spicy flavour profile with a slightly smoky wok hei aroma. The noodles are soft yet retain a slight chew, and the dish is typically garnished with lime wedges and fried shallots. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 18 g protein, 20 g fat, and 68 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of around 1470 mg per serving. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low category) based on yellow wheat noodles as the primary carbohydrate source, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 34 due to the large carbohydrate quantity. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension due to its high sodium load, and requires portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Mee Goreng is a staple at Malay hawker stalls, zi char restaurants, and school canteens throughout Singapore and Malaysia, and is considered a culturally significant comfort food. It is distinct from Indonesian Mie Goreng in its use of tomato ketchup and potato, which give it a sweeter and denser sauce profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mee goreng?
What is the difference between mee goreng and mie goreng?
What noodles are used in mee goreng?
How much sodium is in mee goreng?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for Mee Goreng Malay Style composite dish entries. No single exact match was found for the specific restaurant version (Marina Seafood House, Boat Quay), which is a zi char variant priced at SGD 22 and likely includes seafood or premium ingredients not reflected in standard hawker-centre nutrient profiles. GI is an analogous estimate based on yellow wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD source databases for this dish category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in stir-fried noodle dishes per HPB data. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI=50) based on yellow wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mee Goreng Malay Style was found in the approved sources. 6. The restaurant version served at Marina Seafood House (Boat Quay) at SGD 22 is a premium zi char variant that may include crab or seafood; the nutrient profile here reflects a standard Malay-style mee goreng preparation and may underestimate protein and fat if seafood is included. 7. Halal status is marked true as Mee Goreng Melayu is traditionally a Malay Muslim dish prepared without pork or lard; individual restaurant compliance should be verified. 8. gi_category is 'low' as GI=50 which is ≤55 per the Veritas threshold rule.
